content:
4 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

At first, AI felt… fine.
Interesting. Useful, even.
But not quite what people were hyping it up to be.
So I used it the way most people do:
“Act like an expert in ___.”
Ask a question.
Get an answer.
It worked.
But it felt flat.

Over time, something started to shift.
Not all at once.
Not in some big “aha” moment.
Just… small refinements.
Not just what I was asking —
but how I was asking it.
More context.
Better framing.
A clearer sense of what I actually needed.
And the answers started to change.
They got more specific.
More useful.
More me.

Now, when my sister uses AI, she’ll sometimes say:
“I got some basic info… but can you run it through your way?”
Not because I’m asking wildly different questions.
But because I’m asking them differently.
And once you understand that difference,
a lot more of what AI can do for you starts to open up.

That’s the part many people miss.
They treat AI like a search engine.
But it works more like a conversation with a professional.
If you walked into a doctor’s office and said:
“Something feels off.”
…you wouldn’t expect a diagnosis.
They’d ask:

• What’s going on?

• When did it start?

• What have you tried?

• What matters to you?
That’s the intake form — the part where you give the important details.
Without it, the answer might feel useless.
AI works the same way.

AI gives you safe, generic answers by default.
Not because it has to —
but because that’s what it’s designed to do when you haven’t given it much to go on.
That’s the baseline — not the ceiling.

Think about a new computer.
It comes with default settings.
Standard background.
Basic layout.
It works.
But it doesn’t feel like yours yet.
So what do you do?
You change the background.
Adjust the settings.
Move things around until it fits how you actually use it.
AI works the same way.
The default is just the starting point.

People are hesitant to share that kind of detail.
Sometimes it’s privacy concerns.
Sometimes it’s not knowing what matters.
Sometimes it just feels like too much effort.
So they keep it vague.
And then wonder why the answers feel unhelpful.

I’ve never had an issue sharing details.
That’s just how I am in real life.
I’m not giving out anything sensitive.
But I am willing to say:

• what’s frustrating me

• what I’ve already tried

• what tends to work for me

• what absolutely doesn’t
Not because I have to.
But because it helps me think — and it helps the tool help me.

And here’s what I’ve learned:
You don’t have to share everything.
You just have to share something real.
Dip a toe in.
Instead of:
“What job should I look for?”
Try:

• I like solving messy problems

• I get burned out by repetitive work

• I want flexibility and no travel
That’s not vulnerability.
That’s clarity.

And if you’re thinking:
“I don’t even know where to start.”
Say that.
Literally.
“I don’t know where to start, but here’s what I want to figure out.
Ask me questions that would help you guide me.”
Now you’re not guessing what to ask.
You’re letting the system help you build the intake.

Because this is a skill.
Good project managers do this all the time.
They don’t jump to solutions.
They ask better questions until the problem becomes clearer.
Once you see that pattern, you can use it anywhere.

If you’re trying to find the right job, the right solution, or the right next step…
You have to give the system something to work with.
Otherwise, you’re not filtering anything out.
You’re just getting more noise.

And that’s the real cost.
Not that AI gives you bad answers.
But that it gives you safe ones by default.
The kind that:

• don’t help you move forward

• don’t help you make decisions

• don’t help you avoid the wrong path

AI isn’t bad at helping.
It just reflects what you give it.
And most people are giving it far less than they think.

Note: Share only what you’re comfortable with. And while the intake analogy is helpful, AI isn’t a replacement for licensed medical, legal, or mental health professionals.

About the Author
Jody Blum is a supply-chain and operations professional who finds life lessons in everyday systems — from fulfillment processes to home projects. She writes about curiosity, process, and continuous improvement in both work and life.
